proposal.reasoning: This is the first iteration (exploration phase) with no prior data, so I need to sample diverse regions of parameter space to understand how key parameters affect the target properties (density, heat of vaporization, surface tension, dipole moment). I'm proposing 4 distinct strategies: (1) High Cohesion - testing strong epsilon values to increase thermodynamic properties, (2) Tight Packing - testing smaller sigma values to increase density through better packing, (3) High Polarity - testing maximum charges and bond lengths to maximize dipole moment, and (4) Balanced - a conservative middle-ground approach. This covers the main axes of variation: cohesion strength (ε), particle size (σ), and dipole magnitude (q and bond lengths), while respecting physical constraints (DUN_charge = -DUP_charge, bond force constants > 100, bond lengths < σ/2).

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Changing epsilon values (SUL_epsilon, CGD2_SUL_epsilon) controls cohesion strength where more negative values increase intermolecular attraction, leading to higher density, heat of vaporization, and surface tension. Changing dipole parameters (DUP_charge/DUN_charge and bond lengths CGD2_DUP_bl/SUL_DUN_bl) controls the separation of charge centers where higher charges and longer bonds increase the dipole moment.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: Fork 1 (High Cohesion) will test if strong attractive forces (ε = -1.4, -1.0) improve thermodynamic properties toward targets. proposal.reasoning: The diagnostic report indicates fatal instability with atomic velocities exceeding limits, attributed to excessive dummy bead bond stiffness (300 kcal/mol/Å²) and potential geometric strain. With stuck_counter=6, we must prioritize stability while attempting to improve the dipole moment. The current CGD2_SUL_bl (1.6 Å) is too short to achieve the target dipole moment of 3.96 D; increasing it toward the boundary maximum (1.72 Å) will increase DUP-DUN separation. Simultaneously, reducing the dummy bond stiffness constants (CGD2_DUP_kb, SUL_DUN_kb) from 300 toward the lower boundary (~250) should eliminate high-frequency instabilities. Softening angle force constants from 40 to 34-38 will reduce geometric strain. I propose 4 forks that systematically explore: (1) conservative reduction in stiffness with maximum bond length, (2) aggressive softening with slight charge reduction, (3) intermediate values with angle softening, and (4) near-boundary maximum bond length with moderate stiffness reduction. This diversifies the search while adhering to diagnostic recommendations to escape the crash region.

hypothesis.scientific_rationale: Reducing CGD2_DUP_kb and SUL_DUN_kb from 300 to 250-280 kcal/mol/Å² will reduce high-frequency vibrational modes causing energy conservation failures and velocity limit violations. Increasing CGD2_SUL_bl from 1.6 Å toward 1.72 Å (the boundary maximum) increases the separation between DUP and DUN dummy beads, directly increasing the molecular dipole moment toward the 3.96 D target. Reducing angle force constants from 40 to 34-38 kcal/mol/rad² decreases geometric strain that contributes to numerical instability. Minor charge variations (0.245-0.255) test the electrostatic contribution to stability while maintaining dipole moment direction.
hypothesis.expected_benefit: This will restore simulation stability by eliminating the root cause of atomic velocity explosions (excessive harmonic restraint stiffness), while simultaneously improving the electronic property accuracy by increasing the dipole moment through greater charge separation. The softer angles will allow the molecule to adopt more favorable geometries during thermalization, preventing the strain accumulation that leads to crashes.
hypothesis.test_method: The hypothesis will be tested by evaluating four parallel parameter sets that span the recommended stability space: Fork 1 tests conservative stiffness reduction (250) with maximum bond length; Fork 2 tests aggressive softening (260) with slight charge reduction; Fork 3 tests intermediate stiffness (280) with moderate geometric changes; Fork 4 tests near-maximum bond length (1.71) with balanced softening. Success is defined by completion of simulations without velocity limit violations and improvement in dipole moment toward 3.96 D.
